observation.summary: The proposed Layer B (architectural edges, same-BB sub-kind-parameterised) is intended to be corpus-induced and learnable, while Layer A (epistemic edges, cross-BB) stays human-authored — but the division of labour assumes the two layers are cleanly separable; whether a real deployment will discover an architectural edge that should be elevated to Layer A is unsettled.

warrant.claim: The Layer A / Layer B division should not be treated as architecturally settled until an independently established meta-criterion—external to both layers—explicitly governs when a corpus-induced architectural edge must be elevated to epistemic status, because absent that criterion the boundary is self-certifying and therefore epistemically illegitimate.
warrant.data: The proposed architecture assigns corpus-induction and learnability to Layer B (architectural, intra-BB) while reserving human authorship for Layer A (epistemic, cross-BB), yet the observation concedes that a real deployment may surface an architectural edge that warrants elevation to Layer A. No mechanism external to the two-layer system itself has been specified to adjudicate such elevation decisions; the current design relies on the layer-boundary to police its own integrity.
warrant.warrant: A layered-separation claim is epistemically valid if and only if the criterion used to validate the partition is grounded in a metatheoretic frame that is (i) explicitly distinguished from the object system, (ii) not derived solely from that system's internal axioms or topology, and (iii) acknowledged as provisional relative to any higher metatheoretic frame that may in turn be required. When these conditions are absent, the architecture collapses object-level and meta-level into a single undifferentiated stratum, and the separation claim is self-undermining.
warrant.backing: The warrant against self-certifying boundary conditions is further supported by the principle that epistemic systems cannot self-certify the independence of their own boundary conditions without external or structurally heterogeneous anchoring: where full heterogeneity is unavailable the system must explicitly flag residual circularity and actively solicit adjudication from external vantage points. A corpus-induction process that operates within the same ontological substrate as the edges it might elevate cannot, by itself, supply the required heterogeneity. Additionally, the principle that meta-level principles may adjudicate disputes only when the level-boundary they presuppose is independently established prior to and apart from the adjudication itself entails that any elevation decision made before the boundary criterion is fixed is procedurally premature and potentially circular.
warrant.qualifier: This conservative objection holds with near-certainty as a structural constraint on the design's legitimacy, though it does not entail that a satisfactory external criterion cannot eventually be articulated; it entails only that deployment should be deferred or clearly scoped as provisional until such a criterion is named, located outside both layers, and equipped with concrete procedural triggers for elevation review.

argument_a.evidence: The observation itself is the primary evidence: the designers acknowledge the elevation question is 'unsettled,' which is precisely the condition the warrant identifies as requiring suspension of the boundary claim pending independent meta-criterion establishment. No existing documentation specifies (a) what properties of a corpus-induced edge would be jointly sufficient to trigger an elevation proposal, (b) who or what adjudicates the proposal using criteria not drawn from the two-layer topology itself, or (c) how the decision is recorded and audited independently of Layer A's human-authorship process—confirming that the metatheoretic frame required by the warrant is presently absent.

warrant.claim: The Layer A / Layer B separation is not a stable architectural given but a dynamically negotiated boundary whose negotiation criteria must themselves be housed at a third, explicitly meta-architectural level to avoid collapse into circularity.
warrant.data: The proposed system assigns corpus-induced, learnable edges to Layer B (same-BB sub-kind-parameterised) and human-authored epistemic edges to Layer A (cross-BB), but acknowledges that a deployment might surface an architectural edge that warrants elevation to Layer A—meaning the partition criterion is itself subject to revision by the very evidence the partition is designed to classify.
warrant.warrant: When a finite classification schema is applied to a relational domain of sufficient complexity, any instance that resists assignment to all categories is evidence that it occupies a latent dimension not internalized by the schema. The appropriate inferential response is to record the instance as a boundary artifact with its resistance vector, evaluate whether that vector is idiosyncratic or recurrent, and if recurrent, initiate schema revision by splitting, adding a hybrid category, or elevating a formerly implicit dimension to an explicit axis. Additionally, a layered-separation claim is epistemically valid only if the criterion used to validate the partition is grounded in a metatheoretic frame that is explicitly distinguished from the object system, not derived solely from that system's internal axioms, and acknowledged as provisional relative to any higher metatheoretic frame that may be required.
warrant.backing: Both warrants converge on the same structural demand: a partition boundary that can be contested by the objects it classifies is not a settled meta-level constraint but an object-level artifact that itself requires a meta-level adjudicator. The co-constitutive loop in which Layer B evidence can challenge Layer A assignments is formally identical to the reflexively isomorphic ontology case—where neither prescriptive validity nor descriptive frequency alone can warrant the structure, requiring a frame that takes the co-constitutive loop itself as its explanandum. Without a third level—call it Layer M—that houses (a) the elevation-triggering criteria, (b) the procedural mechanism by which elevation is enacted, and (c) the provisionality acknowledgment that Layer M's own criteria are revisable, the system will silently treat the A/B partition as a logical ground rather than a pragmatic posit, making it structurally self-undermining whenever a boundary-straddling case arises.
warrant.qualifier: This argument holds most forcefully for deployments of sufficient domain complexity that boundary-straddling cases are statistically expected rather than exceptional; in narrow, well-bounded corpora where the A/B distinction maps cleanly onto a pre-established ontological distinction, the need for an explicit Layer M may be deferred, though never permanently eliminated.

argument_b.evidence: The observation itself supplies the primary evidence: the system's designers already anticipate that a corpus-induced edge might need elevation to Layer A, yet no procedural architecture—named elevation triggers, arbitration roles, acceptance criteria—is specified for that transition. The absence of such architecture means that when elevation is attempted, the adjudicating criterion will be drawn from within the very layer structure under revision, instantiating the Level-Separation Condition violation: the inferential mechanism serves simultaneously as the object of the meta-claim and the evaluator of that meta-claim's validity. Analogously, knowledge-engineering literature on ontology evolution (e.g., studies of OWL ontology versioning and modular ontology maintenance) consistently identifies boundary disputes between modular layers as the primary source of uncontrolled semantic drift, precisely because the inter-layer adjudication criteria are housed within one of the competing layers rather than in an independent governance stratum.

contradicts.reason: Claim mismatch: A asserts 'The Layer A / Layer B division should not be treated as architecturally settled until an independently established meta-criterion—external to both layers—explicitly governs when a corpus-induced architectural edge must be elevated to epistemic status, because absent that criterion the boundary is self-certifying and therefore epistemically illegitimate.'; B asserts 'The Layer A / Layer B separation is not a stable architectural given but a dynamically negotiated boundary whose negotiation criteria must themselves be housed at a third, explicitly meta-architectural level to avoid collapse into circularity.'

counter.counter_claim: The attacker's claim that boundary negotiation must be housed at a third, explicitly meta-architectural level does not refute the warrant's conditions but instead defeats the applicability of the warrant altogether: if the negotiation criteria for any A/B partition must themselves reside at a further meta-level, then the warrant's three conditions can never be stably satisfied at any finite level, rendering the warrant inapplicable rather than false.
counter.reason: An undercutter does not deny the warrant's internal logic but removes the ground on which the warrant can be applied. The attacked warrant assumes a stable, finitely-grounded metatheoretic frame can satisfy conditions (i)–(iii). The attacker's claim shows that the very act of designating such a frame requires a further negotiation layer, triggering an infinite regress of meta-levels. This does not show the warrant's conditions are wrong; it shows they can never be jointly met in a real architecture, so the warrant never fires. The circularity charge is thus an undercutting defeater: the warrant is bypassed because its enabling presupposition—that a well-grounded, non-circular metatheoretic frame is achievable—is undermined by the regress the attacker identifies.

pattern.description: The contradiction reveals a structural regularity of infinite regress through meta-level displacement: whenever a warrant's applicability conditions require a governing criterion, locating that criterion at a higher meta-level recreates the same applicability demand at that new level, generating an unbounded regress that never bottoms out. The undercutting move does not falsify the warrant's internal logic but instead exposes that the warrant presupposes a grounded stopping point that the architecture cannot supply. This pattern generalises: any finite-level warrant whose satisfaction conditions include a negotiation or selection mechanism will be undercut—not refuted—if the mechanism is shown to require a further meta-level, because the applicability of the warrant is perpetually deferred rather than denied outright.
observed: self-similar applicability demand at each level

revised_warrant.claim: A warrant whose satisfaction conditions include a selection or negotiation mechanism is applicable only if that mechanism terminates at a grounded, non-circular stopping point that does not itself require a further meta-level criterion.
revised_warrant.data: The structural regularity of infinite regress through meta-level displacement shows that any finite-level warrant presupposing an ungounded selection mechanism is perpetually deferred rather than refuted: each new meta-level recreates the original applicability demand, preventing the warrant from ever bottoming out.
revised_warrant.warrant: If and only if a termination condition for the governing mechanism can be explicitly identified—either as a primitive axiom, a formally self-grounding rule, or a conventionally fixed base case that is not itself subject to further negotiation—then the warrant's applicability conditions are satisfiable in finite steps; otherwise the warrant is inapplicable (undercut) for the current inference cycle and must be suspended until a grounding condition is supplied.
revised_warrant.backing: Foundationalist and coherentist epistemology jointly distinguish vicious from virtuous regress: a regress is vicious when each level inherits the original justificatory burden unmodified; it is benign or terminable only when a level introduces a qualitatively different grounding relation (e.g., self-evidence, empirical immediacy, or mutual support that is not circular). Formal proof theory encodes this distinction via axiom schemas and inference rules that are not themselves derived within the system. Carroll's tortoise regress ('What the Tortoise Said to Achilles') demonstrates that treating inference rules as premises recreates the original demand indefinitely; the standard resolution is to treat at least one rule as a primitive that is applied rather than asserted. These converging analyses support requiring an explicit, level-distinct termination condition before a mechanism-dependent warrant may fire.
revised_warrant.qualifier: Presumptively: a warrant satisfying this revised condition is applicable unless independent evidence emerges that the nominated stopping point is itself covertly mechanism-dependent or circular, in which case the applicability question re-opens at that level alone rather than propagating an unbounded regress.
